quiz.title: High-School Geometry: Unit 10 Quiz 2
quiz.subtitle: Using Base and Height for Triangles; Using Diagonals for Rhombuses
quiz.subject: Geometry
quiz.topic: Geometry: 10.2 Area of Triangles and Rhombuses

questions.question: What is the formula for the area of a triangle with base $b$ and height $h$?
questions.explanation: The area of a triangle is half the product of its base and height: $A = \frac{1}{2}bh$. The height must be the perpendicular distance from the base to the opposite vertex. This formula works for all triangles.

questions.question: A triangle has a base of $14$ cm and a height of $9$ cm. What is its area?
questions.explanation: Area $= \frac{1}{2} \times 14 \times 9 = \frac{1}{2} \times 126 = 63$ cm$^2$. The height must be measured perpendicular to the base.

questions.question: A triangle has an area of $48$ in$^2$ and a base of $12$ inches. What is its height?
questions.explanation: Using $A = \frac{1}{2}bh$: $48 = \frac{1}{2} \times 12 \times h$, so $48 = 6h$ and $h = 8$ inches. Check: $\frac{1}{2} \times 12 \times 8 = 48$ in$^2$. âś“

questions.question: What is the formula for the area of a rhombus with diagonals $d_1$ and $d_2$?
questions.explanation: The area of a rhombus equals half the product of its diagonals: $A = \frac{1}{2}d_1 d_2$. This formula works because the diagonals of a rhombus are perpendicular and bisect each other, dividing the rhombus into four congruent right triangles.

questions.question: A rhombus has diagonals of length $10$ cm and $16$ cm. What is its area?
questions.explanation: Area of a rhombus $= \frac{1}{2}d_1 d_2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 16 = \frac{1}{2} \times 160 = 80$ cm$^2$.

questions.question: A rhombus has an area of $120$ ft$^2$ and one diagonal is $12$ ft. What is the length of the other diagonal?
questions.explanation: Using $A = \frac{1}{2}d_1 d_2$: $120 = \frac{1}{2} \times 12 \times d_2$, so $120 = 6d_2$ and $d_2 = 20$ ft. Check: $\frac{1}{2} \times 12 \times 20 = 120$ ft$^2$. âś“

questions.question: A right triangle has legs of length $6$ cm and $8$ cm. What is its area?
questions.explanation: In a right triangle, the two legs are perpendicular, so one leg can serve as the base and the other as the height. Area $= \frac{1}{2} \times 6 \times 8 = \frac{1}{2} \times 48 = 24$ cm$^2$. The hypotenuse is $10$ cm, but is not needed for area.

questions.question: A triangle has an area of $36$ m$^2$ and a height of $9$ m. What is the length of its base?
questions.explanation: Using $A = \frac{1}{2}bh$: $36 = \frac{1}{2} \times b \times 9$, so $36 = 4.5b$ and $b = \frac{36}{4.5} = 8$ m. Check: $\frac{1}{2} \times 8 \times 9 = 36$ m$^2$. âś“

questions.question: A rhombus and a rectangle have the same area. The rhombus has diagonals of $8$ cm and $15$ cm. The rectangle has a width of $6$ cm. What is the length of the rectangle?
questions.explanation: Area of rhombus $= \frac{1}{2} \times 8 \times 15 = 60$ cm$^2$. Area of rectangle $= l \times 6 = 60$, so $l = 10$ cm. Both shapes have the same area despite different formulas.

questions.question: A triangular sail has a base of $5$ feet and a height of $12$ feet. The fabric costs $\$2$ per square foot. How much does the sail cost?
questions.explanation: Area of the triangular sail $= \frac{1}{2} \times 5 \times 12 = 30$ ft$^2$. Cost $= 30 \times \$2 = \$60$. The perimeter is not needed â€” cost depends on area, not distance around.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have mastered area formulas for triangles ($A = \frac{1}{2}bh$) and rhombuses ($A = \frac{1}{2}d_1 d_2$). You can find missing dimensions and apply these to real-world problems.
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Remember: (1) Triangle area uses perpendicular height, not slant side, (2) In a right triangle, the legs are the base and height, (3) Rhombus area uses diagonals, not side length, (4) Both formulas have a $\frac{1}{2}$ factor.
scoreMessages.50: A fair start. Focus on: (1) the $\frac{1}{2}$ in both triangle and rhombus area formulas, (2) height must be perpendicular to the base in triangles, (3) diagonals of a rhombus are perpendicular, which is why $\frac{1}{2}d_1 d_2$ works, (4) rearranging formulas to find missing dimensions.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! Triangle area: $A = \frac{1}{2}bh$. Rhombus area: $A = \frac{1}{2}d_1 d_2$. Both use half the product of two perpendicular measurements. Always use perpendicular height for triangles, not the slant side. For rhombuses, you need the diagonals, not the side length.
